question: In a distributed system, what is the primary purpose of a consensus algorithm like Paxos or Raft?
options: To ensure all non-faulty nodes agree on a single value or sequence of commands
To increase the overall processing speed of the system
To provide a secure encrypted channel for communication
To automatically partition data across multiple servers
explanation: Consensus algorithms are fundamental to distributed systems for achieving reliability and fault tolerance. Their primary purpose is to get a group of nodes to agree on a single data value or state, even if some nodes fail, ensuring consistency across the system.

question: According to the CAP theorem, a distributed system can only guarantee two out of the three properties. Which are these three properties?
options: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance
Concurrency, Atomicity, Persistence
Consistency, Atomicity, Partition Tolerance
Cohesion, Availability, Performance
explanation: The CAP theorem states that in the presence of a network Partition, a distributed system must choose between Consistency (all nodes see the same data at the same time) and Availability (every request receives a response). Partition Tolerance is the ability of the system to continue operating despite network partitions.

question: What is a key characteristic of the 'eventual consistency' model used in many distributed databases?
options: If no new updates are made, eventually all accesses will return the last updated value
All nodes are guaranteed to have identical data at every moment in time
Writes are always atomic and immediately visible to all users
It requires a global lock to synchronize all read and write operations
explanation: Eventual consistency is a consistency model that does not guarantee immediate consistency after an update. Instead, it guarantees that if no new updates are made to a given data item, eventually all accesses to that item will return the last updated value. This model is often chosen for high availability.

question: In the context of distributed transactions, what is the 'Two-Phase Commit' (2PC) protocol primarily designed to achieve?
options: Atomicity across participating nodes, ensuring all nodes either commit or abort the transaction
High throughput by minimizing network round trips
Immediate read-after-write consistency for all clients
Automatic load balancing of transaction processing
explanation: The Two-Phase Commit protocol is a distributed algorithm designed to ensure atomicity in a distributed transaction. It coordinates all participating nodes so that they all either commit the transaction (if all agree) or abort it (if any node fails or disagrees), thus maintaining data consistency across the system.

question: A system experiences a network partition, splitting nodes into two isolated groups. A client writes new data to the majority partition. According to the CAP theorem, if the system prioritizes Availability and Partition Tolerance (AP), what is the most likely outcome for a client reading from the minority partition?
options: The client may read stale data (a previous value) because it cannot contact the majority to get the latest write
The read will fail or time out because the minority partition is cut off from the latest state
The system will use a quorum-based protocol to automatically synchronize the data before the read
The minority partition will become the new leader and serve the latest written data
explanation: In an AP system (prioritizing Availability and Partition Tolerance), the system remains available to serve requests even during a partition. However, it sacrifices strong consistency. The minority partition, being unable to communicate with the majority, will continue to serve requests but might return stale or older data that does not reflect the latest write performed on the majority side.
